data.title: Rockwell Medical names new finance chief
data.description:
• Rockwell Medical (NASDAQ:RMTI) said that Jesse Neri was promoted to CFO position.
  • Mr. Neri joined Rockwell Medical in October 2023 as SVP, Finance to oversee all aspects of the Company's finance organization. 
  • Prior to joining Rockwell Medical, Mr. Neri was the Executive Director of Finance at Hemavant Sciences and Aruvant Sciences, clinical-stage biopharmaceutical companies.

data.title: Consumer finance watchdog caps bank overdraft fees
data.description:
Big banks will now have to put a limit of $5 on overdraft fees, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau said.

The consumer finance watchdog's new rule could save customers $5 billion annually, and was part of the campaign against junk fees by the Biden administration.

"The CFPB is cracking down on these excessive junk fees and requiring big banks to come clean about the interest rate they're charging on overdraft loans," Rohit Chopra, the agency's director, said in a statement.

Banks and credit unions can charge overdraft fees, but must cap them at $5 or charge depositors a fee that covers the bank's expenses and losses, the statement said.

The new rule applies to banks and credit unions that have at least $10 billion in assets. Previously, customers were charged $35 on an average, when they overdrew their bank accounts.

“For far too long, the largest banks have exploited a legal loophole that has drained billions of dollars from Americans’ deposit accounts,” Chopra said.

The new rule is, however, reportedly opposed by the industry and the Republicans.

More on financials

  • Bank of America CEO sees net interest income moving up the ladder from $14.3B in Q4
  • Former TD Bank employee arrested for allegedly aiding in money laundering
  • Wells Fargo CEO speaks about OCC lifting, Basel III, consumer spending trends, and more

data.title: Nasdaq futures fall after Abobe's outlook disappoints; eyes on PPI
data.description:
Stock index futures traded in the red on Thursday after Adobe issued weaker than expected outlook, and investors will now turn their attention to the wholesale inflation data.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -0.3%, and Dow futures (INDU) -0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.31%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.18%.

U.S. stocks finished mostly higher in Wednesday's history-making session, as an in-line consumer inflation report bolstered expectations of another quarter-point rate cut by the Federal Reserve next week. Nasdaq had ended above 20K, with the Big Tech boosting the index.

"Markets put in a decent performance yesterday, as the U.S. CPI report came in broadly as expected, which was seen as giving the all-clear for the Federal Reserve to cut rates next week," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"When it comes to the Fed, the CPI print left little doubt among investors that another rate cut will happen next week," Allen added.

Adobe (ADBE) shares fell 10.1% in premarket trade after the Photoshop creator issued guidance for fiscal 2025 that fell short of Wall Street's expectations.

Traders are now awaiting the November producer price index data, which will land before the opening bell. Core PPI is expected to come in 0.2% M/M and 3.2% Y/Y.

PPI is expected at 2.6% on a yearly basis, and 0.2% M/M.

The initial jobless claims report is also expected today, and is forecasted to fall to 221K.

More on markets

  • Behind the monster rally that drove the Nasdaq to 20,000
  • CPI Report: Inflation Is No Longer A Concern
  • Reaction Roundup: Experts weigh in on November consumer inflation report
  • The Recipe For A Bull Market

data.title: Commodity Roundup: Commerzbank 2025 copper view; ING sees energy markets under pressure
data.description:
Commerzbank forecasts copper prices (HG1:COM) to reach $9,700 per ton by the end of 2025, while ING sees large parts of the complex edging lower in 2025 with relatively comfortable supply and demand balances.

After rising to just over $10,000 in September, the price slipped back to around $9,000, and are up 8% since the beginning of the year.

The most important driver on the supply side is China, which has significantly expanded its smelting capacities recently and is now likely to account for half of the global supply of refined copper, Commerzbank Research said.

In the last two years alone, the production of refined copper in China has increased by more than 20%, Carsten Fritsch, commodity analyst at Commerzbank noted.

Among relevant base metals producers, Freeport McMoRan (FCX), Southern Copper (SCCO), Buenaventura (EVN), Teck Resources (TECK), Rio Tinto (RIO), BHP (BHP), Vale (VALE), Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF). ETFs: (NYSEARCA:COPX), (CPER)

The sharp rise in copper production in China has meant that imports of unwrought copper and copper products have stagnated for two years and are well below the levels of 2020 and 2021, Fritsch argued.

Copper plays a key role in the electrification of transportation and manufacturing processes, and Commerzbank says demand is likely to remain supported by the political measures to decarbonise the economy.

In addition, the expected support measures from the central bank and politicians are expected to stabilise construction activity, which accounts for a quarter of copper demand.

Elsewhere, the potential for an escalation in trade tensions is a downside risk, ING said, as markets are waiting to see if and when Chinese support measures feed their way into commodity markets in the form of stronger demand.

Looking ahead, analysts expect energy markets to come under pressure.

"The oil market is set to see fairly modest demand growth once again in 2025, which is partly cyclical and partly structural... For now, we expect the oil market to be in surplus next year – although much will depend on OPEC+ production policy."

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Nvidia's request to avoid securities fraud suit dismissed by US Supreme Court - report
data.description:
On Wednesday, The U.S. Supreme Court rejected an appeal by Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) looking to avoid a securities fraud lawsuit by stockholders, who alleged that the company misled investors about how much of its sales relied on the cryptocurrency market, Reuters reported.

The judges, who heard arguments in the case on Nov. 13, decided not to give a decision on the underlying legal dispute and instead dismissed Nvidia's appeal of a lower court's decision which allowed the 2018 class action to go ahead, the report added.

The Supreme Court, in determining that it should not have granted the appeal, kept the lower court's ruling in place, the report noted.

The lawsuit is led by Sweden-based investment management firm E. Ohman J:or Fonder AB.

The Supreme Court's gave no explanation for the dismissal, according to the report.

During the arguments, some of the justices showed reservations about intervening in the case, saying that if there was a clear legal issue for them to decide, as opposed to only a dispute over facts. They also indicated that they were not ideally placed to resolve the lawsuit due to the technical complexities, the report added.

At issue was if the plaintiffs cleared the legal threshold for bringing private securities fraud suits set under a 1995 federal law known as the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act which aimed to filter out frivolous lawsuit.

The plaintiffs alleged that Nvidia and the company's CEO Jensen Huang violated a 1934 federal law known as the Securities Exchange Act by making statements in 2017 and 2018 which falsely downplayed how much of Nvidia's revenue increase came from crypto-related purchases, the report noted.

Starting in 2017, as the price of some cryptocurrencies surged, Nvidia's chips became popular for cryptomining. However, by late 2018 amidst a decline in crypto, Nvidia's revenue did not meet its forecast, leading its stock price to fall in early November that year, the report added.

The lawsuit has requested unspecified monetary damages, partly to regain the lost value of the Nvidia stock held by the investors, as per the report.

In 2022, Nvidia agreed to pay $5.5M to U.S. authorities to resolve charges that it did not clearly disclose the impact of cryptomining on its gaming business, however, without admitting or denying the findings of the regulators, the report noted.

A federal judge had dismissed the stockholder suit, however, the San Francisco-based 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals revived it. The Appeals court found that the plaintiffs had adequately alleged that Huang made "false or misleading statements and did so knowingly or recklessly," permitting the case to go ahead, the report added.

Deepak Gupta, who represented the stockholders before the Supreme Court, said the dismissal was "a win for corporate accountability," as per the report.

Nvidia did not respond to a request from Seeking Alpha for a comment on the matter till the time this report was published.

An Nvidia spokesperson told Reuters that the company is fully prepared to continue its defense. "Consistent and predictable standards in securities litigation are essential to protecting shareholders and ensuring a strong economy, and we remain committed to supporting them," said the spokesperson.

Nvidia said to the Supreme Court that the plaintiffs failed to properly show that the disputed corporate statements were false or that Nvidia had intentionally or recklessly misled investors, as needed by the law, the report added.

In return, the plaintiffs said that the lawsuit has adequate allegations — obtained from former employees, market analysis and expert opinion — to survive the company's request to dismiss the suit, which now moves to the discovery stage of the litigation, the report noted.

The Biden administration had supported the stockholders in the case.

The Nvidia lawsuit was one of the two cases which came before the Supreme Court in November that involved the right of private litigants to hold companies accountable for alleged securities fraud. The other case, which was related to Meta Platforms (META), was argued on Nov. 6 and similarly dismissed by the justices on Nov. 22, the report added.

data.title: U.S. ETFs notch new record as inflows cross $1T this year
data.description:
ETF inflows in the U.S. have crossed a record $1T this year, according to data from consultancy firm ETFGI, with assets invested in ETFs reaching an all-time high of $10.6T at the end of November.

The previous record had been set in 2021, when ETFs saw net inflows of $803.40B and net new assets totaled $919.78B.

The top five ETFs in terms of net inflows YTD according to FactSet: Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) $100.1B, iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:IVV) $61B, iShares Bitcoin Trust (NASDAQ:IBIT) $34.8B, Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (NYSEARCA:VTI) $26.7B, and Invesco QQQ Trust (NASDAQ:QQQ) $26.6B.

The ETF sector has been widely popular among investors around the world, with a record 1,787 new products listed globally in the first 11 months of 2024. This represents a net increase of 1,234 products after accounting for 553 closures, ETFGI noted. Of the new launches, 646 ETFs were in the U.S.

The top three new ETFs in terms of assets: iShares Bitcoin Trust (NASDAQ:IBIT) $48.43B, Grayscale Bitcoin Trust (GBTC) $20.99B, and Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC) $19.28B.

According to BlackRock's (BLK) iShares unit, 2024 is on track to be a record year for global ETP (exchange-traded products) flows, with $1.6T added YTD, surpassing the previous record of $1.3T in 2021. Notably, YTD flows into U.S. equity ETPs reached $547B on November 4, and then rose to $690B by the end of November.

data.title: Nordson stock slides after projects Q1 and FY25 outlook below estimates on weak farm equipment demand
data.description:
Nordson (NASDAQ:NDSN) stock down 6% in extended trade, triggered by weak forecast for Q1 and full year, while Q4 quarter results tops estimates.

The Westlake, Ohio-based company reported fourth quarter 2024 results with sales of $744 million, up 4% year-over-year, though organic sales declined 3%. Analysts currently expect revenues of $736.79 million.

Q4 net income declined to $122 million from $128 million year-over-year and adjusted earnings were $2.78 per diluted share, up from $2.71 in the prior year. Analysts currently expect adjusted earnings of $2.59.

Key positives include strong EBITDA margins at 32% and robust free cash flow conversion at 105%.

By Segment: Advanced Technology Solutions' sales of $152 million increased 5% in Q4 compared to the previous year, primarily due to a 4% organic sales increase and a 1% favorable currency impact, primarily in test and inspection product lines.

Industrial Precision Solutions reported $392 million in sales, a 3% decline from last year, due to a 5% decrease in organic sales, offset by 1% gains from acquisitions and currency impacts.

Medical and Fluid Solutions sales increased 19% in Q4 due to Atrion's acquisition, despite a 3% organic sales decrease and 1% favorable currency impact. The organic sales decrease was primarily due to softness in medical interventional solutions product lines, with modest growth in medical fluid components and fluid solutions.

Looking forward, the company provided fiscal 2025 guidance with sales projected between $2,750-$2,870 million (midpoint $2.81 billion) and adjusted earnings of $9.70-$10.50 (midpoint $10.10) per diluted share. Analysts currently estimate revenues of $2.92 billion and adjusted earnings of $10.37 for fiscal 2025.

Nordson expects first quarter revenues in the range of $615 to $655 million (midpoint $635.0 million), with adjusted earnings in the range of $1.95 to $2.15 per diluted share (midpoint $2.05). Analysts currently expect revenues of $676.54 million and adjusted earnings of $2.27 for the quarter.

"The fiscal first quarter is seasonally Nordson’s weakest quarter due to the holiday and calendar year-end slowdowns and cautious customer spending. While we remain confident about the long-term growth drivers of our end markets, we are being prudent about our expectations for end market recovery timing, particularly for our electronics and agricultural product lines," said Nordson President and Chief Executive Officer Sundaram Nagarajan.

data.title: Quantum-Si expands international distribution network, stock up
data.description:
• Quantum-Si (NASDAQ:QSI) Thursday said that it has expanded its international distribution network to a total of 15 partners.
  • QSI  (QSI) stock up +7.81% premarket to $2.07.
  • The commercial distribution network includes further expansion across Western Europe, Eastern Europe and Asia, as well as new expansion into the Middle East, Africa, South America and the South Pacific regions.
  • “We have made significant progress in expanding our international distribution network over the last several months, assembling an outstanding network of partners that are well positioned to drive adoption of Platinum,” Jeff Hawkins, Chief Executive Officer of Quantum-Si said.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Computershare to buy London and Poland-based ingage IR
data.description:
Computershare (OTCPK:CMSQY) to acquire ingage IR—investor relations and engagement software provider to help listed companies build and manage their functions.

The company has clients in 15 countries globally and was included in the FT1000 ranking of Europe’s fastest-growing companies in 2023 and 2024.

ingage’s team is based in London and Wrocław in Poland, and Computershare said it expects all staff members to transfer as part of the deal.

“Acquiring ingage is an important step in growing and developing the suite of services that we offer listed companies, including greater support for investor relations teams and engagement with their shareholder registers." said Fiona Chalmers, global Head of Issuer Services at Computershare.

The transaction is expected to close by end of the year.
